doing that, um I'm going to talk about
the AI opportunity matrix real quick
here. The idea if you are trying to
figure out where do I start, what should
I use AI for, this is where I point
everybody. This is the AI opportunity
matrix and what I recommend you do is is
plot on this quadrant here
all of the tasks that you do and how
frequent they are. So you can see low
frequency, high frequency, and then how
much time they take. A lot of time or
low time. So if something takes a lot of
time
and is very low frequency, odds are this
is very much a human activity. Now,
could you use AI to make yourself do
this faster? Sure, but it doesn't come
up that often to build like a full
system, which you're going to hear me
say a lot by the way. I believe in AI
systems.
Um
may might not make most sense to start
there. But if it's high frequency and
high time, gosh, that's your multiplier
zone. This is where you can get a lot of
your time back by using AI and you can
think through where you can plug AI in
in order to make these things happen
quicker. If it's high frequency low
time, this is great because hey, it
doesn't take us that much time, but it
happens really frequently. And those
typically are simpler tasks that AI is
really good at helping you automate. And
then finally, the low time low
frequency, I always joke and say why are
these even here? That's the That's the
quadrant's name. Um cuz these are things
that just don't happen very often, and
um, they don't take much time. It's
like, why are you even doing these
things? Okay, so that's the AI

just so you know, is

important and also a little tricky.
If you've ever had a prompt that was
working well for you and then changed
the model
and noticed it didn't work all that
well, then you know the problem that
we're talking about here. Just because
your prompt works with
uh Claude Summit 4.6, doesn't mean it's
going to go and work well with Gemini
3.1 Pro.
Um
and then vice versa. You might have a
prompt that works really well for 3.1
Pro, but does not work very well for
some of the other models. So, there's
some con- context that you need in order
to how to do um you know, how to prompt
for specific things for specific models.
And I want to bring this up first.
There's a couple of definitions I want
to give you before we dive into the
actual material. So, we have the prompt
itself.
We have the input. So, the prompt is
something that you type in, okay? Um
this would be the set of instructions
uh that describe the task and how you
want the AI to complete it. This might
be something you literally copy and
paste in because you're doing a specific
task, or if you're building an AI agent,
this would be more of a system prompt
that would live within the AI agent, and
then you would use it like you know,
normal words and whatever little
prompts, and it would use its system
prompt in order to execute the task. We
can dive more in detail on that in a
minute. And if you type in your
questions, I will get to them. Okay,
input. These are the details you give
the AI along with the prompt. A call
transcript, marketing materials, and
other specifics. And this is something I
want to make sure you understand.
I have a prompt. Within that prompt,
there's an area where I expect the
person using the prompt to add details
that will be used by the AI to complete
the task that it's trying to complete.
That's called an input, that one little
area. And then there's an output, which
is what the AI delivers back to us.
We're all very familiar with that. We
have an output. So, it's important you
understand this language here because
there's a lot of different ways of
speaking about this. It also depends on
what type of system that you're you're
working with. Is it a
kind of AI automation chain, or is it
more of an agentic workflow?
These words all interchange, so this is
how I'm using them today, just so you
know.
Prompt is the full prompt. Input is the
part of the prompt that you would
change. And then the output is the
output that you get back from that
prompt. So, those are our definitions. I
just want to be clear about that. Now, I
have
a handful of steps here,
four of them, on writing effective
prompts. I'm going to give you an
overview of the four, and then I'm going
to break down what an effective prompt
looks like, and then I'm going to give
you a prompt to write these prompts.
And so, the first one is the the
directive. Yeah, I call it the
directive. This is the goal you're
giving the LLM, large language model, um
to complete for you. So, three things
you need to know about this. In my
directive, I want to be very clear on
what I'm asking the LLM to do, and the
instructions it needs in order to do it.
This is a part of prompting that so many
people miss.
Excuse me. This is the part of pro
prompting that so many people miss,
which is you want to be very clear about
the steps the AI should take to get to
the output that you're looking for.
Literally, step one, do this. Step two,
do this. Step three, do this. Step four,
do this, etc. The idea here is could I
give this set of instructions to a
junior employee, maybe at an intern that
that I hired out of college. They can
follow those steps and get to an output
that I like. If you can't do that with a
junior employee hire,
your prompt isn't good enough for the
AI. It's not nearly as smart as a human
is. Not even close.
Next, here I have focus your
instructions on the sequence of actions
required to complete a task. So, you
think of the task that you're asking the
LLM to do for you. Maybe it's writing an
email. Maybe it's um
uh creating a ICP document or a
competitive battle card. How would you
go about doing that? What's the sequence
of actions that you would take? And make
sure it's very clear about what those
sequence of actions look like. And then
finally, be very specific about what you
want the AI to do.
Self-check, this is what I was talking
about earlier. If I give these exact
instructions to a junior colleague,
could they complete the task without
asking for clarification? Now, I want to
show you a bad example of a directive.
Bad.

consolidate all of the client's details
and create a report that includes all of
the details about the clients and the

and what AI isn't. An AI is not a
magical
system
that knows how to operate all of these
things like you think it does, right?
You think like, "Oh gosh, I say like, oh
can you send an email to so-and-so?" It

within a harness as it it lives as a
tool used to complete stuff. So, you
have this LLM and then between the LLM
and everything the LLM is capable of
doing, there's something called
let's just call it the application
layer.

happens is we have you.

what you want is something completed.
Okay, let me just say yes, very much.
Yes, okay, good, good. I'm glad
I'm glad this is helpful for folks.
Okay.
Um
So this is the human, okay? So I want
you to understand what's happening here
is you think you're talking directly to
an LLM. This would be like Chat GPT.
You kind of are, but not really. What
you're really doing is you're chatting
in a chat interface. That chat interface
goes through an application layer.
And this here is your query.
You ask a question of the LLM. I'm going
to make the application layer a little
bit bigger here.
I'm going to move these over cuz I want
to show you something very interesting.
A lot of people do not realize this, and
that's why I want to share this with
you.
Um I Side note, if this is your first
time coming to live classes, this is the
type of stuff I like to do. This is not
your normal like you're not seeing this
on Instagram when you're scrolling, but
it's very important that you understand
this.
You are not talking directly to LLM.
You're talking to the LLM through an
application layer. The application layer
allows the LLM to do things based on the
query that it gets. So what happens is I
say, "Hey, I would like you Let me do
like a an actual more challenging one.
Okay. I would like you to schedule an
appointment on my calendar for tomorrow
at 3:00 with my friend Leo, okay?"
If you send it in as your query, the
application layer picks it up and sends
it directly to the LLM. And the LLM,
because of its system prompt, which
lives here, we talked about system
prompts earlier,
that because of the system prompt,
it
Let me get this looking right.
It knows it gets these kind of queries.
This is why prompts are important. Now,
every LLM has a system prompt. Even
ChatGPT. If you're using ChatGPT, you're
using Claude. When you type something
in, it's getting plugged into a system
prompt and it's running it through the
LLM. Okay, it's important to understand
that, too. Cuz you're like your prompt
that you're giving that you think of as
a prompt is actually a prompt that lives
within a prompt. It's kind of
interesting. But the LLM has a system
prompt that says, "Hey, you may get
asked to book appointments on calendars.
Here's some instructions on how to
handle those types of queries." Okay.
So, human says, "Schedule an
appointment."
The application layer feeds that
directly to the LLM. There's some nuance
there that I'm going to leave out
because I want to keep this simple and
we'll get back to our scheduled
programming. Okay.
The LLM cannot go into your calendar and
schedule an appointment without using a
tool.
But the LLM can't just doesn't have
hands. They can't just use a tool.
All the LLM does is predict the text
that comes next and give it to you.
So, what ends up happening based on its
system prompt is it uses what's called
the tool. And that tool has instructions
on what the LLM needs to output the text
in order to do the job of scheduling an
appointment. Okay? So, what happens is
the LLM gets your query to schedule an
appointment, has the tool to schedule
the appointment that requires it to
output text in a weird code-like format,
giving instruction around uh what time
it should be, who should be on the
invite, uh and all of these different
details. And it wraps it up in a little
syntax, so it says like schedule tool,
and then what it wants scheduled in a
really code-looking format, and then end
/schedule tool, right? When it outputs
that, it doesn't output it to you.
Instead, it outputs it just like it
normally would, but it gets caught by
the application layer. The application
layer goes, "Oh, I see the syntax that
the LLM is currently using a tool.
That's not meant to go to the human."
The application layer is hard-coded to
recognize that that tool exists, and
uses it, parses the information, and all
with code goes to your calendar, which
we'll do here, uh whoops, for
we'll do here. This is going to be your
calendar, your very nice, friendly
calendar.
And the application layer goes, "All
right, this was actually an a calendar
thing. We do the calendar."
When that's done,
it outputs that it's done. This is again
all code, no AIs involved in the
application layer. It picked up, because
of the syntax,
that the LLM was trying to do something
in a calendar, parsed it because it
knows how to parse it because of the
instructions the LLM gives it, our
instructions to tell it how to format
the text in a way the application layer
can work with. The application layer
goes,
does the thing because it's coded it's
hard-coded to do that thing, and then
says, "Thing has been completed."
Because it knows that it's been
completed because it just did the thing.
When the LLM gets the thing has been
completed from the application layer, it
goes and does this.
"Perfect. I have gone and scheduled that
appointment. Human, are you happy with
me?" And you go, "Yes, I'm very happy.
Thank you, magical AI system."
But a lot happened in order to get
there. So, I'm going to pause here.

helpful to understand? Is this new
information? Just give me a little
feedback. Make sure we're on the right
track.

side of this and I'm going to show you

thing in a minute.
But just give me a little bit of
feedback while we're doing that.
All right. So, why does this matter for
data? Okay, great. Um well, why this
matters is because based on the
information you give it, in our example
here,
there's a system prompt and there's also
these tools, and usually the tools
the tool description lives in in the
system prompt and then it can call
certain tools.
The tools give instructions on how to
use it, right? And then, um when you
have that instruction, LLM follows the
rules, right? This is context. This is
data.
Okay? So, you want to be thoughtful
on what data you give it. So, let me
give you a bad and a good example.
Bad example, okay?
Here's a copy of a book on marketing.
Use this book to help me write great
email copy.
Bad. Don't do this.
And it's bad because you're asking for
one specific thing. You're asking for an
email
being written.
You're giving
the AI an entire book on marketing
to write a great email for you. It
doesn't need all that information. And
what you're asking the AI to do is
review all of that text. And we're going
to get into context window in a minute.
Next, we have data.
Or sorry, next we have the good example.
Here's a section of the book about
marketing that provides a framework for
writing great email copy.
That's what you want to give it. Because
if you give it that,
it can actually follow that framework
and you're not relying on it to find it.
Now, to get to context here, an LLM has
something that's called a context
window. Okay, that's what I was going to
use this for.
Context window
is simply
how much text
can the LLM handle at once? And when I
say handle, I keep it in its brain.
You and I can hold a lot of text in our
brain at once. We can kind of hold all
of the text we've ever had in our brain
all at once.
Uh
AI can't really do that. Okay, the LLMs
have a limit. And for a long time, the
most common limit, I think it's still
the most common limit now, is about
200,000
tokens. And just think of a token as a
word. I'm going to use the term word
now, but know that they're tokens.
Um
it can only handle 200,000 words. And
that's like
four books.
Four or five books of of text, right?
Um
Now,
newer LLMs can handle up to a million
tokens. So, it's like a crazy amount.
And you might think, "Oh, the fact that
it can handle more words and remember
more words at once or work with more
words at once means it's probably fine.
And like you can just give it the whole
book and who cares cuz it's within the
context window."
Um if you've ever had the experience
where you're talking to an AI and it
feel it seems like it forgot stuff that
you mentioned earlier, that's because
that stuff was too much text and fell
out of the context window. And they've
sort of solved this somewhat in the
sense to where LLMs can now handle more
context. So, they do uh compaction of
context to try to keep threads going
longer. Um but what I want you to
understand you really need to know is
that just because the context window is
higher or larger, right, does not mean
that it doesn't matter what you put in
there. Because the more context you're
giving it, the more the LLM needs to
analyze that that uh text and determine
what is actually relevant. Remember,
these this guy here, the LLM, is not
actually smart. It is auto complete. And
I say that a lot and and it is a little
bit disrespectful because it it it is
miraculous what you can do when you have
a very strong auto complete. But I do
not want anyone here to believe it's
anything other than that. That's it. It
just is auto complete. And when you give
it a huge book and say, "Hey, write me
an email marketing." what happens is
that part of the system prompt, the
instruction, tells the LLM, "First you
must think about what you are trying to
do." And then what the LLM does is it'll
output tokens saying like, "Oh, I got a
whole book on marketing. The user is
asking for an email.
I should look at I should find just
parts of the book that are email
oriented." There's this whole back and
forth thing that's happening.
Okay? Very important to understand that.
That's the reasoning part. And we
figured that out probably like a year
and a half ago, maybe maybe 2 years ago.
The big innovation of Deep Seek, if you
remember when Deep Seek, this is a year
ago, I think. Deep Seek came out and
like blew everything up, right? The
whole
the stock market crashed, all the AI
stocks crashed. One of the big
innovations they found was this
reasoning stuff where you can just have
the LLM like output stuff and then work
with it and they keep doing it and they
loop this reasoning um before giving a
final output.
Um
this is important for you to understand
because if you give it too much text and
hope that it finds the thing, you're
hoping hoping that it finds the thing.
If you can just give it the thing, you
get much better outputs.
That's why what I say,
it's not good give an entire book on
marketing when all you're asking for is
an email. Just give it the framework on
email that you want to do. And context
engineering is the process of going
through and deciding what context the AI
needs within that prompt to actually do
that job well.
All right.
I'm probably going a little too advanced
here.
So just can you give me a yes if you're
following? Just like give me a quick
temperature check here while I go
through number three. Step number three
here is design. So, this is actually the
structure of your prompt. And the great
way to think about this, similar to what
I was talking about earlier, is a good
prompt in design is one that follows a
logical structure that a human can look
at and be able to follow as
instructions.
And the second one here is defining all
the rules about what not to do and what
to do. And number three here is organize
your prompts with the most important
details at the beginning and the end.
Okay, let me check in here. So, for the
most part people are getting it. So, I'm
losing some of you. So, I want to do one
more thing here that I think you'll find
helpful. salesthread.ai/um

I think is what it's called.
Okay. So,

why that's important. Again, and if

writes prompts for you. So,
I want to explain these as concepts so
you're learning and just getting better.
Um
then we're like you'll have the easy
button of this, okay? I will give you
the easy button. But, for now I want to
make sure you're at least starting to
get this information. Okay. So, this is
the loss in the middle thing. There's a
great paper that came out last year
called loss in the middle and it talks
about how LLMs tend to prioritize
context in their context window.
And here's an example. Salesforce, which
is my my new company, it's called Sales
Thread. I I highly recommend you do a
demo if you have a sales team and you
want to do better coaching.
Uh what we do is we review sales call
transcripts. We do a whole bunch of
analysis with a bunch of different AI
agents on those sales call transcripts
and we use that to construct memory of
that deal as well as selling memory for
your organization. What works, what
doesn't work, and also how the reps are
performing on specific skills
on a call that are predictive of deals
closing, so that way we could track
those things and they can improve it.
That's what we do. And the core thing is
based around transcripts. So, a
transcript come in and as a human, if
you're listening to a sales call, you'll
spot like, "Okay, we're talking about
budget here. Uh you know, I'm not really
sure sure if this is the right buyer.
Oh, here's a pain point that just came
up, right?" And we can track this pretty
easily. And if you were to sit down and
you listen to four sales calls with one
deal, you'll have a really good sense as
a human of what the risks are to the
deal, and you'll have a sense like how
likely is this deal to close, right?
And as you add more transcripts, you're
going to be able to keep managing that
context by adding more transcripts,
adding more transcripts. Well, the LLMs
don't work that way. They typically
prioritize things in the beginning and
the end of their context window. So,
what I'm showing here is like green,
gray, I'm really focused there. Green,
I'm really focused. And we kind of lose
in the middle. And what it does is it
makes it just a little bit harder to
spot those things.
And as you get closer to the top, it's
easier to see. As you get closer to the
bottom, it's harder to see.
All right. That's why context is
important. And how you design your
prompt matters because the more stuff
you put into a prompt, the more
challenges you run into
with getting the prompt to do the task
you want well.
And I bring this up because I find that
a good amount of people will have one
prompt that they want to do a lot of
things.
So, I want you to do uh I want you to
research this lead, and then I want you
to take the main uh
solutions that they provide. This is my
competitor. I want you to analyze G2. I
want you to find all of the um
the bad reviews that they had, and then
I want you to look at the features and
tie to the bad review, and then create
me a report, and then look at our
website, and then boom boom boom, and
now I want a competitive battle card
that compares you and us, or them and
us.
That's a lot in one prompt. And what
ends up happening is you shove a lot of
context in there, a lot of instructions,
the AI loses some stuff, you're fighting
this context battle, and the outputs
typically get worse.
I've had prompts that are 22 pages that
perform incredibly well.
I've had some of those prompts that had
to stay that big. I've had other prompts
that I've cut it all down to four pages
and it performed better.
And I'll show you an example in a bit
here. Um so I want you to understand
what's going on here. All right, to go
back to the design portion, here is how
I recommend
organizing the prompts. And actually,
you know what? Let me I have a better um
I have a better version of this. Let me
just pull this up for you, too, cuz I
think um I think I illustrated it a bit
better here.
Um okay. Pop this out.
Yeah, this is better.
Ah.
That got huge.
Okay.
Center view. All right, great. So let's
do this uh real quick here.
This is an image about prompt design.
This is an example. So you can see I
have that primary directive at the top.
That's a very important thing because
it's basically the high-level this is
what we're trying to accomplish with
this prompt. Then I have the context
here. Hey, here's the section of the
marketing email or the marketing book
about writing strong promotional emails.
Here are the steps I want you to take to
craft the promotional email. Here are
some examples of what good promotional
emails look like, and you want to be a
bit careful with examples, by the way,
but they should be in there.
Rules and reminders.
And then output formats. And the rules
and reminders generally point back to
the steps, and the primary directive
references the steps, as well.
And that's how you kind of keep things
reined in a bit, in my experience. So
that's the structure of a prompt. I'm
going to go back here.
I'm going to actually exit out of that
just so I don't have a thousand tabs
open, which kind of already do, but
Okay. So, that's how uh this works.
Let me move this way.
Uh and no, I'm not a Star Trek fan.
Um
Yeah, they do have something like that,
like they have prime prime directive.
Uh there was a study a while ago. A
buddy of mine brought London who was the
one who turned me on to calling it that.
Uh but there's a study a while ago where
you got better outputs from LLMs if you
used uh sci-fi references.
Uh and I think that's where it
originates from. Okay, so the last thing
here is develop. Again, I'm going to
give you some examples of this in a
minute, but the last thing here is
develop.
And this is looking for how your prompt
could be better. You have to test these
things.
So, you want to look for how your output
outputs could be better. You want to
make small improvements to the prompt or
the context to improve your output. And
then I want you to focus on one
improvement at a time.
And this is what it looks like. You run
the prompt, you review the output.
You run it again with the changes that
you made. You make more changes.
And you repeat this process over and
over and over and over again.
Okay. So, I'm going to pause here.
If you have questions, type them into
the chat, but what I actually want to do
here
is I want to show you a prompt that I
have.

I have this document, but let's just do
simple version.

it was, I think.
Yes.
Okay, this is a This is a different
example.
This is a prompt that I use. I'm going
to show you this. Here's a great example
to copy my writing style.
Okay? Uh if AI doesn't write like you,
that's a user problem. AI very well is
possible to have write like you, okay?
Um so
one of the things that I have done is I
have worked on this prompt. This one
here is
Okay, it's not actually 60 pages. One
second.
Let me get to the bottom of it. There's
multiple prompts here cuz I was testing
stuff. Okay, but I wrote this prompt.
Let me just walk you through it
somewhat quickly here. I wrote this
prompt to replicate how I write. And as
you can see here, it's very long. I have
the objective. I have what I'm going to
be providing, who you're writing as, I
see important notes, my voice and tone
rules, and I give all of these details
about how I write the best that I can,
kind of text that I like, and I give
clear instructions to analyze what you
what needs to happen, identify what
needs to be changed here,
write in the right voice. And as you can
see, I keep going through and I do a
voice check. I have all these clear
instructions and I keep going. I give
examples of good and bad. Good, bad,
good, good, bad, bad. I feel like giving
it so many examples. We're 24 pages in
here and this thing keeps going. I give
examples of how I use humor because in
my writing, I don't want it to use bad
humor. I want it to use humor that's
kind of like how I use humor and not try
to like ham it up and say funny stuff
that's not actually funny. Um
and I give examples of what it would
look like for blog posts, for emails,
for writing social posts. You can see,
this is a really detailed prompt. In
fact, it might actually be 60 pages now
that I'm scrolling through this.
Um holy crap, this is 60 pages. This is
a 60-page prompt. So, this is a super
complicated prompt.
Let's do a word count.
Oops.
Okay.
This is 6,000 words. That's almost a
chapter of a book, by the way.
A normal blog post like 3 to 4,000
words. Basically, double the blog post
just to try to get it to rewrite a piece
of text like I write.
Now,
I went and I simplified that prompt.
I simplified it down to 18 pages. So,
60% or 60% reduction.
You can see who you're writing as here,
the voice rules.
This is in voice, this is not in voice.
I give some examples there.
And then the user input. You can see
this is like way simple, way more simple
than what we had before.
Right? I actually even cut the steps
here cuz I thought it was overkill. It's
cuz pretty is a one-step thing. Anyways,
you can see much simpler. Then I tested
a bunch of outputs. So, this was like an
email to promote a live class that I was
doing.
Or I actually don't know what this email
is for. You see there was a
Yeah, yeah, this was for like hiring
sales reps or something.
Here's the output for the complex.
You can see this little thing, and then
here's the output for simple.
And simple, I'll give you the punchline
here, just wrote way more like how I
naturally write. And it did it every
single time. This is like
I don't know, some kind of post,
probably LinkedIn post. This is the
complex output, this is the simple
output. And in every single example,
the simple output won.
So, less instruction, better output.
Pretty amazing.
And here's what I've learned, and this
is probably like the biggest piece of
learning that I've learned recently,
probably over the last 8 months,
is that there is two different types of
tasks you're asking AI to do. There's an
analytical task, and there's a judgment
task. A judgment task is make a judgment
on whether this is the right way of
writing something, for example, or um
make a judgment on if this is the right
direction I should go. And then the
other type of task are the analytical
task. And analytical tasks are things
like
"Hey, review this and tell me if it
meets this criteria."
What I have found is that when I have a
judgment task, I actually want to not
give so many examples and instead give
more principles. Principles around what
good looks like, what good doesn't look
like. Versus here's something that's
good and here's something that's bad.
And the reason why is because for
judgment-based tasks, I want to use the
power of the LLM, which is predicting
what text should come next.
And if I give too many examples,
what ultimately ends up happening
is
I get worse outputs
because it pattern matches too much on
the examples. Because it has so many
examples, it starts pattern matching and
I get weird outputs. And that's why the
writing styles
work so much better with a simple prompt
versus a complicated prompt. Because I'm
asking it to use judgment on how I would
write,
not analytical.
Okay.
So, that's a breakdown of an actual
prompt. We've covered a lot today. Your
head might be spinning a little bit, but
hopefully you felt like you've leveled
up. Actually, if you feel like type in
what number you feel like you are now
and is it higher than the number you did
earlier, right? We did it at the
beginning of this live class, we talked
about the numbers. Um we got a question
that I'm going to answer. Oh, quick plug
actually since you're all here.
Um Sales Thread is my new company. If
you run a sales team, um
what we are building is a tool that
creates the memory layer for AI agents
and for humans around what works in your
sales process, what doesn't work. And
the byproduct of that is you get really
accurate forecasts and you get a lot of
insights on what coaching has to happen
for your sales reps in order for them to
improve their performance. And
meanwhile, we're learning and we give a
suite of AI agents to your sales people
so you can do more with less. The goal
is how do I have a small sales team that
can handle a lot more deals? And then
there's all these great byproducts of
it. That's the real real high level. So,

Let me
Let go through the questions. The first
one here is from Stephanie.
What do you think of asking AI to
actually write your prompt for you based
on what you would have written to
improve it? So, yes, you absolutely
should do this. So, one of the things
Excuse me. One of the things I love to
do when I'm working on a new prompt is I
use a prompt to write the first draft,
then I test if that prompt is working,
and I find a set of things that I don't
like about the output. So, maybe it if
I'm uh using it to write uh marketing
emails, I'll go, "Oh, I don't like this
part of the marketing email."
And then I will collect my feedback and
say like, "Hey, I do not like these
outputs for these reasons. Why do you
think that is and what parts of our
prompt could we change in order to get
the outputs to be better?" I have it
make the recommendations. I pick one of
the tweaks or two of the tweaks or keep
it kind of small, make those tweaks,
then do another round of testing and see
if we're improving.
Okay. Great question, by the way.
Uh clarification, was that last prompt
you showed a better version of the one
you provided a link for? No, the prompt
I was just walking you through was a
complicated version and a simple version
of
my own um kind of write like Kyle

which I will repost a link to the prompt
writing prompt here.

All right. Any other questions? I'm
going to kind of wind down here over the

questions we will hop off early.
Um
a lot of information today. I know it's
a lot. And the prompt engineering stuff,
I think if at a bare minimum if there's
just one thing you can walk away with,
use pick a task that you want to get AI
to do really well, use the prompt
writing prompt to get your first draft
of the prompt. Part of that is going to
have a section that it wants examples.
Do not give more than three examples
of what good looks like. That's it.
Then run that prompt five times with
five different things.
With those five things, those outputs,
look at them and say, "What do I like?
What don't I like?" And then ask the AI
what it recommends you change in order
to have less of what you don't like.
Make a few tweaks, run the prompt again.
And let's just all focus on getting one
prompt that works really well for you to
do one thing.
